claim_text: Routine screening mammography for women aged 40-49 at average risk reduces breast-cancer mortality.

gold_sources.citation: Independent UK Panel on Breast Cancer Screening (Marmot review). The Lancet (2012).
gold_sources.supports: Concludes screening reduces breast-cancer mortality (~20% relative) across invited age ranges, accepting overdiagnosis as a cost.

gold_contradict_sources.citation: Gøtzsche PC, Jørgensen KJ. Screening for breast cancer with mammography. Cochrane Database Syst Rev (2013).
gold_contradict_sources.contradicts: Cochrane review questions the mortality benefit, emphasizing trial-quality issues and substantial overdiagnosis; benefit in the 40-49 group is least certain.

gold_contradict_sources.citation: USPSTF breast cancer screening recommendation history (2009/2016).
gold_contradict_sources.contradicts: Graded the 40-49 benefit as small/uncertain and made routine screening in that band an individual decision rather than a blanket recommendation.

distractor_sources.note: The 40-49 band specifically is where guideline bodies disagree most; older bands have clearer benefit. Verdict hinges on the age qualifier.

notes: Authoritative bodies reach opposite practical conclusions for this exact age band from largely the same trials — a textbook `mixed` evidence base, not insufficient evidence.
